methods.name: Stage-Progression
methods.rationale: Applies typical Series A → next-stage step-up multiplier (2.0x) to $900M total raised proxy.

methods.name: Peer-Median
methods.skip_reason: insufficient narrow peer set in Nuclear Energy (found 1 same-stage, 1 adjacent-stage, 0 similar-tier; need ≥3)
methods.rationale: 8 same-sector peers exist in Nuclear Energy, but none of the narrow filters (same-stage / adjacent-stage / similar-valuation-tier) yielded ≥3 datapoints. Falling back to all-sector median would be misleading.

public.rationale: Public-co frontier-tech comp in Nuclear Energy

Public-co frontier-tech comp in Nuclear Energy

private.name: Fuse Energy
private.rationale: Same sector (Nuclear Energy), same stage (Series A), post-money $200M+

data.narrative:
Pacific Fusion, founded by Eric Lander and Will Regan, is a Series A–stage nuclear energy company that has raised $900M to date. The valuation exercise references a peer universe of 11 companies across nuclear energy—3 public (OKLO, NNE, SMR) and 8 private players including Fuse Energy, TerraPower, X-Energy, and Kairos Power.

Stage-progression methodology is the primary applicable approach. Using total capital raised ($900M) as a proxy baseline and applying a 2.0x step-up multiplier typical of Series A progression yields an implied valuation of $1.8B. Peer-median triangulation was not feasible; while 8 same-sector private comparables exist, filtering for stage, adjacency, and valuation tier produced insufficient datapoints (fewer than 3) to support a robust median approach, avoiding false precision from the broader all-sector cohort.

The analysis supports a valuation range of $1.5B (low) to $2.1B (high), with $1.8B as the median. This range reflects application of a single methodology and carries material uncertainty: the stage-progression multiplier assumes typical capital efficiency and milestone achievement for Series A nuclear ventures, which may not hold if Pacific Fusion's capital deployment, regulatory pathway, or technical de-risking materially deviates from peer norms. The absence of sufficient narrow-set comparables limits cross-validation. Investors should treat this range as indicative pending deeper operational and technical due diligence.
